title: Ounces to Grams Cooking 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Convert ounces to grams instantly: oz × 28.35 = g. Free online calculator for cooking and baking. No signup required.
intro: This calculator converts between ounces (oz) and grams (g) for cooking and baking, using the exact internationally recognized conversion factor of **1 oz = 28.3495 g** (avoirdupois ounce). Whether you're scaling a recipe from a US cookbook to metric measurements or following a European pastry formula in a US kitchen, precise unit conversion is critical — especially in baking, where a 5–10 g difference in flour or sugar can noticeably change texture and structure. The calculator works in both directions: oz → g and g → oz, covering the most common ingredient weights used in professional and home cooking.
keyTakeaway: **1 fluid oz ≠ 1 weight oz.** For dry ingredients: **grams = ounces × 28.3495**; for the reverse: **ounces = grams ÷ 28.3495**. Example: 3.5 oz of bread flour = 99.2 g.
useCases: Scaling a US recipe that calls for 4 oz of butter into grams for a kitchen with a metric digital scale
Converting a French pastry recipe that specifies 250 g of bread flour into ounces for a US cook without a metric scale
Portioning 16 oz (1 lb) of ground beef into individual 4 oz (113 g) burger patties with precision
Measuring spice blends accurately when a rub recipe lists 0.5 oz of smoked paprika but your scale reads only in grams
Comparing nutrition labels: a US label shows a serving size of 1 oz (28 g) of cheese, confirming the correct portion weight
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The conversion uses the **avoirdupois ounce**, the standard unit for food and commerce in the United States (defined by NIST):

```
# Ounces to Grams
grams = ounces × 28.3495231

# Grams to Ounces
ounces = grams ÷ 28.3495231

# Example: 6 oz of all-purpose flour
grams = 6 × 28.3495 = 170.10 g

# Example: 500 g of bread flour
ounces = 500 ÷ 28.3495 = 17.64 oz
```

> ⚠️ **Do not confuse with the troy ounce** (31.1035 g), used for precious metals — never for food.

---

## Reference Table

| Ounces (oz) | Grams (g) | Common Cooking Use |
|---|---|---|
| 0.25 oz | 7.09 g | Pinch of yeast packet |
| 0.5 oz | 14.17 g | Half a tablespoon of butter |
| 1 oz | 28.35 g | 1 serving size (cheese, deli meat) |
| 1.5 oz | 42.52 g | Standard shot of spirits |
| 2 oz | 56.70 g | Small chocolate square |
| 2.5 oz | 70.87 g | Typical egg (large) |
| 3.5 oz | 99.22 g | Small chicken breast |
| 4 oz | 113.40 g | ¼ lb burger patty |
| 6 oz | 170.10 g | 1 cup of all-purpose flour |
| 8 oz | 226.80 g | 1 cup of water / ½ lb |
| 12 oz | 340.19 g | Standard can of beer/soda |
| 16 oz | 453.59 g | 1 pound (1 lb) |
| 32 oz | 907.18 g | 2 pounds |

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1: Baking bread — converting a US recipe to grams
A sourdough recipe calls for **12.3 oz of bread flour**. Your kitchen scale only reads grams:
```
grams = 12.3 × 28.3495 = 348.7 g
```
You measure **349 g** of bread flour — accurate enough for professional baking (±1 g tolerance).

### Case 2: Converting a French recipe to ounces
A croissant dough recipe specifies **250 g of cold butter**. Your scale reads in ounces:
```
ounces = 250 ÷ 28.3495 = 8.82 oz
```
You measure **8.8 oz** of butter. Note: for laminated doughs, a ±2 g variance (≈0.07 oz) is acceptable.

### Case 3: Portioning protein for meal prep
A nutrition plan calls for **4 oz cooked chicken breast** per meal. You're cooking 7 portions:
```
Total = 7 × (4 × 28.3495) = 7 × 113.4 g = 793.8 g raw
```
Account for ~25% cooking weight loss → purchase **~1,058 g (≈37.3 oz ≈ 2.33 lb)** raw chicken.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using volume ounces (fl oz) for weight** — 1 fl oz of water ≈ 29.57 g, but 1 fl oz of honey ≈ 42 g due to density differences. Always use weight ounces for dry and semi-solid ingredients.
2. **Rounding too aggressively** — Rounding 28.3495 to 28 introduces a 1.2% error. Over 10 oz, that's a 3.5 g shortfall — enough to affect cookie spread or bread hydration.
3. **Confusing avoirdupois and troy ounces** — The troy ounce (31.1035 g) appears on some antique kitchen scales. Using it for flour would mean 9.6% more ingredient than intended.
4. **Assuming "1 cup = 8 oz by weight"** — 1 cup of all-purpose flour weighs ~4.25 oz (120 g), not 8 oz. The 8 oz = 1 cup rule applies only to water and similar-density liquids.
5. **Ignoring tare weight when using a bowl** — Converting correctly but forgetting to zero/tare the scale before adding the ingredient negates the precision of the conversion entirely.

---

## Related Calculators

- Explore volume-to-weight conversions at [Cups to Grams Cooking](/conversion-cups-grams-cooking)
- Work with larger quantities using [Pounds to Kilograms](/conversion-pounds-kilograms)
- For liquid measures, see [Milliliters to Fluid Ounces](/conversion-ml-fl-oz)

faq.q: What is the exact conversion factor from ounces to grams?
faq.a: The exact factor defined by NIST (the US National Institute of Standards and Technology) is **1 avoirdupois ounce = 28.349523125 grams**, established under the international yard and pound agreement of 1959. For cooking purposes, rounding to 28.35 g is precise enough for virtually all recipes.

faq.q: Is there a difference between a fluid ounce and a weight ounce in cooking?
faq.a: Yes — and it's a critical distinction. A fluid ounce (fl oz) measures **volume** (≈29.57 mL), while a weight ounce measures **mass** (28.35 g). For water, they're close: 1 fl oz of water ≈ 29.57 g. But 1 fl oz of honey ≈ 42 g because honey is denser. Always check whether a recipe uses weight or volume ounces — US recipes often mix both.

faq.q: How many grams is 1 oz of the most common baking ingredients?
faq.a: All-purpose flour: 1 oz ≈ 28.35 g (but 1 cup ≈ 120–125 g, or ~4.4 oz). Granulated sugar: 1 oz ≈ 28.35 g. Butter: 1 oz ≈ 28.35 g (= 2 tablespoons). Salt: 1 oz ≈ 28.35 g. These weight-based conversions are consistent — unlike volume-based cup measurements, which vary by ingredient density and how the cup is filled.

faq.q: Why do professional bakers prefer grams over ounces?
faq.a: Grams offer finer resolution and easier scaling. A gram is roughly 1/28th of an ounce, so a metric scale can detect differences that an ounce-based scale misses entirely. Most professional kitchens and culinary schools in the US have shifted to grams because a 1 g error in a 500 g batch of bread dough (0.2% variance) has far less impact than a 1 oz error in the same batch (~5.6% variance).

faq.q: How do I convert ounces to grams without a calculator?
faq.a: Use the quick mental shortcut: **multiply ounces by 28, then add 1% of that result**. Example: 5 oz → 5 × 28 = 140, plus 1% of 140 (1.4) ≈ 141.4 g (exact: 141.75 g). The error is under 0.25%, acceptable for home cooking. For baking, always use a calculator or scale for precision.

faq.q: Does the type of ingredient affect the ounce-to-gram conversion?
faq.a: No — for **weight** measurements, 1 oz of any ingredient is always 28.35 g regardless of what it is. However, if a recipe uses **volume** ounces (cups, tablespoons), then density matters enormously. For example, 1 cup of water = 8 fl oz ≈ 237 mL ≈ 237 g, while 1 cup of bread flour = ~4.25 oz by weight ≈ 120 g.

faq.q: What kitchen scale precision do I need for ounce/gram conversions?
faq.a: For everyday cooking, a scale with **1 g / 0.1 oz resolution** is sufficient. For professional baking or confectionery (chocolate tempering, macarons, bread hydration ratios), use a scale with **0.1 g resolution**. The USDA recommends scales with at least 1 g precision for accurate portion control in food service. Scales accurate only to 5 g are inadequate for spice or leavener measurement.

faq.q: How do ounce-to-gram conversions apply to nutrition labels?
faq.a: The FDA requires US nutrition labels to list serving sizes in both household measures and metric (grams). A label showing '1 oz (28 g)' uses the standard avoirdupois conversion rounded to the nearest gram. When tracking macros or calories, use the gram value directly — it's more precise. For example, a 1 oz serving of almonds = 28 g = approximately 164 calories, 6 g protein, 14 g fat.
